md: If exponentials answer 'how big does it get?', **logarithms** answer the reverse: 'how many doublings does it take?' A logarithm is just an exponent in disguise.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.body: $\log_b(x) = y$ means $b^y = x$. In words: 'the log base $b$ of $x$ is the exponent you raise $b$ to in order to get $x$.'

md: Two bases get their own shorthand: **$\log(x)$** with no subscript usually means base 10 (the **common log**), and **$\ln(x)$** means base $e$ (the **natural log**).
They turn multiplication into addition, division into subtraction, and exponents into multiplication. Three rules carry all the weight:

These follow directly from how exponents work. Example for the product rule: $b^{m+n} = b^m \cdot b^n$, so taking logs: $m + n = \log_b(b^m \cdot b^n)$, i.e. $\log_b(b^m) + \log_b(b^n) = \log_b(b^m \cdot b^n)$.

blocks.title: Identities worth memorizing
blocks.body: $\log_b(1) = 0$ (because $b^0 = 1$). $\log_b(b) = 1$ (because $b^1 = b$). $\log_b(b^n) = n$ (the log and exponent cancel). $b^{\log_b(x)} = x$ (same — they're inverse operations).

md: **Bring down an exponent.** $\log(x^3) = 3\log(x)$. Useful for solving equations like $x^5 = 100$: take logs of both sides to get $5 \log(x) = \log(100) = 2$, so $\log(x) = 0.4$, then $x = 10^{0.4} \approx 2.51$.
Most calculators only have $\log$ (base 10) and $\ln$ (base $e$). If you need a log in some other base, use:

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: $\log_b(x)$ is just the exponent that takes $b$ to $x$. Three rules: products turn into sums, quotients into differences, exponents into multipliers. Use $\log$ for base 10 and $\ln$ for base $e$. The change-of-base formula handles anything else.
